conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me first review the official eligibility rules to ensure I give you accurate guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who's 18 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 18 years old.

Since Alex is over 16, their eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training. Could you please tell me: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in education. He's studying for his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. A-levels would typically count as approved education.

To confirm your eligibility, I need to ask: **Does Alex live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me, and I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

**Are you currently receiving Child Benefit for Alex, or is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for him?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not currently receiving Child Benefit for Alex, and nobody else is claiming it for him either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:
- Alex is 18 years old and studying for A-levels (approved education)
- Alex lives with you in the UK (Manchester)
- You are responsible for Alex
- Nobody else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex

According to the UK government rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16. However, you'll still qualify if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. A-levels count as approved education.

Since Alex is 18 (under 20), lives with you, and is in approved education (studying for A-levels), you meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Since Alex is 18 years old, under 20, and in approved education (A-levels), and lives with you in the UK, you meet all the eligibility requirements.

You can proceed to apply for Child Benefit. The eligibility will continue as long as Alex remains in approved education or training and is under 20 years old.

Please note: If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, but you can still claim the benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 18 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and studying for A-levels. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, but you'll still qualify if your child is under 20 and stays in approved education or training. Alex meets this criterion as he is under 20 and in approved education (A-levels). Alex lives with the claimant in the UK (Manchester), establishing that the claimant is responsible for him. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (18 years old), who lives with them in Manchester and is studying for A-levels. Since Alex is under 20 and in approved education, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit under the extended eligibility rules for children aged 16-19 in education or training.

No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (18 years old), who lives with them in Manchester and is studying for A-levels. Since Alex is under 20 and in approved education, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit under the extended eligibility rules for children aged 16-19 in education or training.
